fact: November 5 is exactly 309 days into the year, which means there are only 56 days left until New Year's Eve!
In the Northern Hemisphere, this is prime time for squirrels to go absolutely nuts burying acorns - they can bury up to 10,000 acorns in a single season!
The sun sets before 5 PM in many northern cities around this date, making it feel like bedtime when you're just getting home from school.
November 5 falls smack in the middle of Scorpio season, when people born under this zodiac sign are said to be extra mysterious and intense.
In Australia and other Southern Hemisphere places, November 5 is actually spring cleaning time - flowers are blooming and baby animals are being born!
This is peak leaf-peeping season in many places, when maple trees turn colors so bright they look like they're on fire.
November 5 is roughly halfway between the autumn equinox and winter solstice, making it a perfect balance point in the year's darkness.
Many bears in cold regions are getting seriously sleepy around this time, eating up to 20,000 calories per day to prepare for hibernation.
The first frost of the year hits many temperate regions right around November 5, turning spider webs into glittery crystal art overnight.
Geese flying south create their famous V-formations most actively during early November, and you can hear their honking from over a mile away.
In northern latitudes, the aurora borealis (northern lights) become more visible around this time because the nights are getting longer and darker.
November 5 marks the time when many deciduous trees have lost about 75% of their leaves, creating crunchy carpet layers up to 6 inches deep.
